1.2:
14-15. sword, and ... bow--

for any instruments of violence. literally, "slaughter" ( 1Sa 25:11 ). God's people ( Psa 10:17 ; 12:5 ). The punishment of the wicked as drawn on themselves--often mentioned (compare Psa 7:15 , 16 ; 35:8 ).
1.8: 18-19. God, who knows His people's changes, provides against evil and supplies all their need.
1.14:
23-24. steps--

way, or, "course of life"; as ordered by God, failures will not be permanent.

2.1: 27-29. The exhortation is sustained by the assurance of God's essential rectitude in that providential government which provides perpetual blessings for the good, and perpetual misery for the wicked.
2.7: 34. On the contrary, the good are not only blessed, but made to see the ruin of their foes.
2.13:
39-40. strength--

( Psa 27:1 ; 28:8 ). straits ( Psa 9:9 ; 10:1 ). In trust and quietness is the salvation of the pious from all foes and all their devices.
2.19: 1-4. He deprecates deserved punishment, which is described ( Psa 6:1 ), under the figure of bodily disease [Psa 38:3].
2.25: 5-8. The loathsomeness, corruption, and wasting torture of severe physical disease set forth his mental anguish [Psa 38:6]. It is possible some bodily disease was connected. The loins are the seat of strength. His exhaustion left him only the power to groan [Psa 38:9].

3.4: 13-14. He patiently submits, uttering no reproaches or replies ( Joh 19:9 ) to their insulting speeches;
3.10: 18. Consciousness of sin makes suffering pungent, and suffering, rightly received, leads to confession.
3.22:
make me to know mine end--

4-7. Some take these words as those of fretting, but they are not essentially such. The tinge of discontent arises from the character of his suppressed emotions. But, addressing God, they are softened and subdued. experimentally appreciate. literally, "when I shall cease."

4.5: 8-10. Patiently submissive, he prays for the removal of his chastisement, and that he may not be a reproach.
4.11:
inclined--

1-3. The figures for deep distress are illustrated in Jeremiah's history ( Jr 38:6-12 ). Patience and trust manifested in distress, deliverance in answer to prayer, and the blessed effect of it in eliciting praise from God's true worshippers, teach us that Christ's suffering is our example, and His deliverance our encouragement ( He 5:7 , 8 ; 12:3 ; 1Pe 4:12-16 ). (the ear, Psa 17:6 ), as if to catch the faintest sigh.

5.6: 11. may be rendered as an assertion, that God will not withhold ( Psa 16:1 ).
5.12:
love thy salvation--

16. (Compare Psa 35:27 ). delight in its bestowal on others as well as themselves.
5.18:
poor--

1-3. God rewards kindness to the poor ( Pr 19:17 ). From Psa 41:2 , 11 it may be inferred that the Psalmist describes his own conduct. in person, position, and possessions.

6.3: 7-8. So of others, all act alike.
6.9:
11-13. favourest--

or tenderly lovest me ( Ge 34:19 ), evinced by relief from his enemies, and, farther, God recognizes his innocence by upholding him.

7.2:
4. The verbs are properly rendered as futures, "I will remember," &c.,--

that is, the recollection of this season of distress will give greater zest to the privileges of God's worship, when obtained.
7.8: 9-10. in view of which [Psa 42:8], he dictates to himself a prayer based on his distress, aggravated as it was by the cruel taunts and infidel suggestions of his foes.
7.14:
2. God of my strength--

by covenant relation my stronghold ( Psa 18:1 ). in scorn. or, "in," that is, in such circumstances of oppression.
7.20:
have told--

1-3. This period is that of the settlement of Canaan ( Jos 24:12 ; Jud 6:3 ). or, "related" (compare Ex 10:2 ).
7.26: 6-8. God is not only our sole help, but only worthy of praise.

8.2: 11. The Babylonian captivity not necessarily meant. There were others (compare 1Ki 8:46 ).
8.8: 16. Its cause, the taunts and presence of malignant enemies ( Psa 8:2 ).
8.14:
stretched out ... hands--

20-21. A solemn appeal to God to witness their constancy. gesture of worship ( Ex 9:29 ; Psa 88:9 ).

9.2: 23-26. This style of addressing God, as indifferent, is frequent ( Psa 3:7 ; 9:19 ; 13:1 , &c.). However low their condition, God is appealed to, on the ground, and for the honor, of His mercy.
9.8:
sword--

3-4. The king is addressed as ready to go forth to battle. (Compare Re 1:16 ; 19:15). (Compare Is 9:6 ). generally used as divine attributes ( Psa 96:6 ; 104:1 ; 111:3 ), or as specially conferred on mortals ( Psa 21:5 ), perhaps these typically.
9.14: 9. In completion of this picture of a marriage festival, female attendants or bridesmaids of the highest rank attend Him, while the queen, in rich apparel ( Psa 45:13 ), stands ready for the nuptial procession.

10.1:
14-15. The progress of the procession is described; according to the usual custom the bride and attendants are conducted to the palace. Some for the words--

propose another rendering, "on variegated (or embroidered) cloths"--that is, in the manner of the East, richly wrought tapestry was spread on the ground, on which the bride walked. As the dress had been already mentioned, this seems to be a probable translation.
10.7:
1. refuge--

literally, "a place of trust" ( Psa 2:12 ). ( Psa 18:2 ). literally, "a help He has been found exceedingly." as in Psa 18:7 .
10.13:
earth melted--

6. (Compare Psa 46:2 ). all powers dissolved by His mere word ( Psa 75:3 ; Ho 2:22 ).

11.4:
4. He shall ... inheritance--

the heathen to be possessed by His Church ( Psa 2:8 ), as Canaan by the Jews. literally, "pride," or, that in which he glories (not necessarily, though often, in a bad sense), the privileges of the chosen people-- His love being the sole cause of granting them.
11.10:
throne of ... holiness--

8-9. The instruction continued. or, "holy throne" (see on Psa 2:6 ; Psa 23:4 ).

12.5:
4-6. For--

The reason is given. Though the kings (perhaps of Moab and Ammon, compare Psa 83:3-5 ) combined, a conviction of God's presence with His people, evinced by the unusual courage with which the prophets (compare 2Ch 20:12-20 ) had inspired them, seized on their minds, and smitten with sudden and intense alarm, they fled astonished.
12.11:
9. thought of--

literally, "compared," or considered, in respect of former dealings. in acts of solemn worship (compare 2Ch 20:28 ).
